title: Searching the Space of Models
concept: NAS works by searching a defined space of possible architectures, where choices can include block type, layer depth, channel width, kernel size, skip connections, and other structural options.

exercises.prompt: Recap: NAS usually searches over what?

options.text: A human-defined set of possible architecture choices
Every imaginable neural network without limits
Only image labels, not model structure

exercises.explain: Right — the search needs a space to search in. Now let's unpack what that space can contain.

cards.title: A model family can be treated like a box of building pieces
cards.body: Instead of viewing a CNN as one fixed blueprint, NAS can treat it as a family of possible blueprints. The search process asks questions like: how many times should this block repeat, how wide should this stage be, and should this path include a skip connection?

cards.title: What is a search space?
cards.body: A search space is the set of architectural choices the algorithm is allowed to explore. In CNNs, that might include choices about depth (how many layers or blocks), width (how many channels), block type, kernel size, and connections between parts of the network.

cards.title: Like assembling a toolkit for different jobs
cards.body: If you are packing tools for a repair job, you might choose between a small wrench or large wrench, one flashlight or two, and a ladder or no ladder. You are not choosing from every object on Earth — only from a curated toolbox. NAS works the same way: it explores combinations from a prepared set of useful parts.

cards.title: Small structural choices can create many candidates
cards.body: Imagine three stages of a CNN. Each stage can use either a standard block or a mobile block, can be repeated 2 or 3 times, and can be narrow or wide. Even with just those few choices, the number of possible architectures grows quickly. That is why a clear search space matters.

cards.title: A bigger search space is not automatically better
cards.body: It is tempting to think 'more options must mean better results.' But a huge search space can become slow, noisy, and hard to search well. A smaller, well-chosen space can be more useful than an enormous one full of bad ideas.

cards.title: What NAS might be allowed to vary
cards.body: Different NAS setups choose different knobs, but these are common ones.

left.text:
Block type
Depth and repeats
Width / channel count

right.text:
Skip or no skip
Kernel size choices
Stage layout or branching

exercises.prompt: Which set of choices sounds like part of a CNN NAS search space?

options.text: Whether to use 3×3 or 5×5 kernels, how many blocks to repeat, and how wide each stage should be
Whether the office coffee should be hot or iced
Only the file name of the dataset zip
Whether to delete the validation set entirely

exercises.explain: Right — those are real structural choices a search process might explore.

exercises.prompt: True or false: in NAS, the search space can include different widths, depths, and connection patterns.
exercises.explain: True — those are classic architecture knobs.

exercises.sentence: A NAS search ___ defines which architecture options the algorithm is allowed to explore.
exercises.explain: Exactly — the search only works inside the options you provide.

exercises.prompt: Which design question most directly belongs in a CNN NAS search space?

options.text: Should this stage use 64 or 128 channels?
Should the classroom walls be painted blue or green?
Should a PDF be stored in landscape orientation?

exercises.explain: Yes — channel width is a direct architecture design variable.

exercises.prompt: A team defines a NAS system where each CNN stage may choose between two block types, two widths, and two repeat counts. What is the main reason this setup is called a search space?

options.text: Because the algorithm will explore combinations of allowed architecture choices inside those boundaries
Because the team is searching for missing image labels instead of model designs
Because all CNNs become identical once width is chosen
Because the search space guarantees the cheapest model is always the most accurate

exercises.explain: Exactly. NAS needs a menu of allowed structural choices, and even a small menu can create many candidates. Next: why searching all those candidates can get very expensive.
